preFix.title: Replacing a gate valve with a ball valve
preFix.subtitle: Swap out a worn gate valve on a domestic cold or hot water pipe
preFix.excerpt: How to isolate a section of pipework, remove an old gate valve that no longer seals fully, and fit a replacement compression-end or push-fit ball valve, including draining the section, cutting out the old valve, preparing the pipe ends, and testing the new valve.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Pegler Yorkshire product installation guides. CIPHE (Chartered Institute of Plumbing and Heating Engineering) guidance.

content.text: A
gate valve
that no longer seals must be replaced rather than repacked: the seating inside a corroded gate valve is past repair. Replacing it with a
ball valve

of the same bore (15 mm or 22 mm) is the standard repair. Before starting, identify the upstream isolation point: usually a gate valve or stopcock on the rising main or tank outlet: and confirm it fully seals before cutting anything.
Step 1, isolate and drain

Close the upstream isolation point. Open a nearby tap to drain down the section of pipe. Hold a cloth under the area to catch residual water. Confirm the pipe is dry before cutting. If the old gate valve is seized and cannot be removed by unscrewing, the pipe must be cut on both sides of the valve using the pipe cutter.
Step 2, remove the old gate valve

If the old gate valve has compression fittings at each end, undo both compression nuts with the adjustable spanner, holding the valve body to prevent it rotating. Withdraw the valve. If the valve is soldered (capillary fittings), cut the pipe on each side of the valve with the pipe cutter, leaving enough pipe protruding to fit the new
compression fittings
: at least 15 mm of straight pipe is required. Polish the pipe ends with emery cloth to bright copper.
Step 3, prepare the pipe ends

Polish both cut pipe ends with the emery cloth until bright. Deburr the inside of the pipe end with the deburring tool (usually a pin on the back of the pipe cutter). The pipe end must be perfectly round: a slightly oval end from a worn cutter will prevent the olive seating correctly.
Step 4, fit the new ball valve
Slide a compression nut and then a new brass olive over each pipe end. Insert each pipe end into the
ball valve

compression socket until it meets the pipe stop inside. Slide the olive up to the fitting, thread on the compression nut, and hand-tighten. Then tighten with the spanner one full turn beyond hand-tight: do not overtighten. Ensure the ball valve handle is in the closed position before restoring the supply.
Step 5, test and check

Restore the upstream supply slowly. Watch both compression joints for drips for at least 2 minutes. If a joint weeps, tighten a further quarter turn. Open the ball valve handle (in line with pipe) and confirm flow downstream. Close the ball valve (crossways to pipe) and confirm flow stops. A ball valve that passes water when closed indicates the olive was not compressed correctly: drain, dismantle, fit a new olive, and refit.
Variations

Push-fit ball valves (Speedfit, Hep2O) are fitted without olives or solder: the pipe end is pushed in to a depth marked on the pipe and is retained by a toothed grab ring. They are removable with a release collar and are the fastest joint to make in an emergency. They are not suitable for pipework in concealed locations: only use push-fit in accessible positions where a future leak could be found and remedied quickly.

attrs.fix: Isolate and drain. Undo the nut and check the pipe end position — it must be hard against the stop. Refit with a new olive (never reuse a compressed olive) and tighten one full turn past hand-tight.
attrs.cause: Olive not sufficiently compressed; pipe end not inserted fully into the fitting socket.
attrs.symptom: Compression joint weeps when supply is restored

attrs.fix: Check the valve is installed with the flow direction arrow matching the pipe flow. Flush through before closing if debris is suspected. If the body is distorted by overtightening, the valve should be replaced.
attrs.cause: Valve fitted crossways to the flow direction; debris in the valve body; valve body slightly overtightened at one end, distorting the body.
attrs.symptom: Ball valve handle is stiff and requires force to turn

attrs.fix: Isolate, remove the valve, inspect the ball and o-rings. If the o-ring is damaged, replace it from the valve manufacturer's repair kit. If the valve is defective, return and replace.
attrs.cause: Ball seat o-ring damaged during installation; valve supplied with a defective seal.
attrs.symptom: New ball valve passes water in the closed position

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
paragraph reads at grade 11.3 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
